SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claim—that modeling contextual dependencies among utterances with LSTM variants improves multimodal sentiment/emotion classification—is supported by experiments reported in Tables 4–6 showing consistent gains over a uni-SVM baseline and prior work (Section 4.2 and Table 5). The paper gives sufficient implementation detail for the feature extractors and LSTM architecture (Sections 3.1–3.2), but it omits statistical significance testing and full training/split details (only partial training procedure notes and Algorithm 1 references), which are minor gaps that do not invalidate the main conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies LSTM-based context modeling to multimodal utterance-level sentiment, which is a reasonable and useful idea, but conceptually it is an incremental combination of known components (text-CNN, 3D-CNN, openSMILE, and LSTMs) rather than a wholly new algorithm (Sections 3.1–3.3). The hierarchical fusion framing and systematic comparison of uni/bi/trimodal contextual variants offer some novelty, but the core techniques are extensions of established methods cited in Related Work (Section 2).

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized into standard sections (Related Work, Method, Experiments) and provides concrete model descriptions (text-CNN, 3D-CNN, openSMILE, contextual LSTM in Sections 3.1–3.2). However, the manuscript contains repeated/duplicated text blocks, some missing algorithm detail (Algorithm 1 is referenced but not fully shown in the excerpt), and occasional typos which reduce readability (multiple repeated paragraphs in Section 4.5).

SUBSTANCE.justification: Experiments are reasonably comprehensive: multiple datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP), unimodal/bimodal/trimodal ablations, comparisons of sc-/bc-/h-LSTM and hierarchical vs non-hierarchical fusion, plus a cross-dataset generalization study (Sections 4.2–4.4 and Tables 4–6). The paper discusses qualitative failure cases (Section 4.5) and reports many implementation details (feature dims, CNN/filter sizes), but lacks statistical tests and some full reproducibility details (exact splits, hyperparameter search protocols).

IMPACT.justification: The work addresses an important and practical problem—multimodal sentiment/emotion at utterance level—and demonstrates consistent improvements over baselines and cited prior methods (Section 4.2 and Table 5), so it is likely to be useful to researchers building contextual multimodal systems. It is not a radical paradigm shift, but the hierarchical/contextual fusion contributions could be widely adopted in the subfield.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (multimodal sentiment analysis using deep contextual models) and the methods (CNNs, LSTMs, multimodal fusion) are squarely within the scope and expectations of top NLP/ML venues, and the paper evaluates on standard community datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP), making it a strong fit (Sections 3 and 4).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to a reasonable set of baselines including uni-SVM, state-of-the-art methods by Poria et al. and Rozgic et al., and evaluates many internal variants (Tables 4–6 and Section 4.2). Some comparisons raise fairness questions (the authors note Poria et al. did not use speaker-independent evaluation) and no statistical significance is reported, but overall the empirical comparisons are relevant and informative.
